danger_signals.meaning: Rebels can defeat army and storm Presidential palace; indicates loss of popular support and imminent regime threat
Army can stage coup d'état; visual state likely shown through unit behavior or alert indicators
Cold War superpowers (US/USSR) will launch invasion to overthrow regime; critical threat to continuation
Military bases established on island may overthrow El Presidente if satisfaction drops
Island populace votes leader out of office if approval is negative; electoral threat to power

safety_signals.meaning: Soviet Union and United States supply foreign aid money when satisfied; indicates stable external support
Foreign military base on Tropico protects island from rival superpower and offers monthly payments
Population content with regime; reduces threat of electoral removal or rebellion
Individual factions aligned with regime prevent disruption of El Presidente's rule

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Will invade if unhappy with regime
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: May establish military base on island; builds infrastructure when allied

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Will invade if unhappy with regime
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: May establish military base on island; offers foreign aid when satisfied
Active in regions with low support; can assault Presidential palace

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Defects to launch coup if dissatisfied
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Loyalty tracked through satisfaction meter; presence at palace and garrisons

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Presence of US and Soviet military options, ideological alignment choices in edicts and policies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Superpowers offer aid, build bases, and threaten invasion based on diplomatic stance
Citizens vote against El Presidente in elections; increased rebellion activity; faction defection

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Military base structure on island; foreign aid indicators
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Monthly payments received; protection from rival superpower; potential military oversight
Multiple threat vectors active: rebels advancing, army considering coup, superpowers threatening invasion
Factions satisfied; population content; able to execute edicts without major opposition

environmental_storytelling.example: Military bases physically placed on island represent superpower control and protection; visible symbol of external political alignment
Choice of El Presidente (real figures like Castro, Che Guevara, or fictional characters) sets political identity and initial faction attitudes toward player
Martial law, tax breaks, and praise edicts visually/mechanically communicate regime policies and their immediate impact on world state
End-game scoring based on citizen happiness, economy health, Swiss bank account, and difficulty signals the moral/pragmatic outcomes of governance choices

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Support regime in elections; low rebellion activity; stable productivity
Vote against leader; join rebel forces; may stage uprising
Defend palace; prevent coups; execute orders
Stage coup d'état; may refuse orders; threat to Presidential palace
Provide foreign aid; may establish protective base; offer monthly payments
Launch invasion; threaten regime; withdraw support
Provide foreign aid; may establish protective base; offer monthly payments
Launch invasion; threaten regime; withdraw support
Assault Presidential palace; defeat army units; threaten regime stability
Demand specific tasks; overthrow El Presidente if satisfaction drops

notes: Tropico communicates world-state primarily through mechanical/gameplay systems rather than explicit visual design. The game relies on edicts, faction satisfaction meters, and scripted events (invasion, coup, election) to manifest political and danger states. Visual manifestation is minimal in available documentation; the game emphasizes strategic decision-making and consequence simulation over environmental visual storytelling. Cold War setting provides implicit visual framing (1950-2000 timeline, Caribbean island aesthetic, ideological choice presentation). Character customization (real vs. fictional leaders) acts as narrative framing for initial faction relationships.
